findings.title: Unexpected exceptions escape main() as a raw multi-frame Python traceback; no --debug flag gates the trace
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: A first-run operator who passes a bad --items / --human-labels path, hits a packaging/import fault (e.g. a missing scientific dep), or trips any pre-panel-loop error gets a wall of Python stack frames dumped to their terminal. The actionable structured error (e.g. CalibrationLoadError's [INPUT_PATH_MISSING] ... (hint: ...), or OllamaUnreachableError's [OLLAMA_UNREACHABLE] ... (hint: ollama serve / OLLAMA_HOST)) IS present, but only as the LAST line under the traceback, dressed as a crash. The operator is not strictly stuck (the hint is reachable if they read to the bottom), so this is MEDIUM not HIGH — but a config-shaped or environment-shaped problem is presented as an internal failure, eroding trust in a research instrument that bills itself on legible measurement.
findings.description: main() in cli.py contains no top-level try/except. The only guarded path is _version()'s PackageNotFoundError. The console-script entry (pyproject [project.scripts] ai-crucible = ai_crucible.cli:main) and __main__.py both do `raise SystemExit(main())`, so any exception raised inside characterize_main BEFORE the run_panel per-model loop -- load_items/_load_admission_items (CalibrationLoadError), _parse_models, load_human_labels, OR the lazy `from ai_crucible.characterize.run import ...` import at cli.py:65 -- propagates as a raw traceback. There is no --debug/-v flag to opt INTO the trace, which means the trace is shown unconditionally (the opposite of the desired default: clean error for operators, full trace only on --debug). Note the daemon-down-mid-run case is already handled: OllamaUnreachableError raised inside judge_item is caught by run_panel's per-model except and a total-failure run returns the structured CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS exit-1 (run.py:673-688). The gap is specifically the pre-loop / unexpected-exception path at the cli.py layer. This is the exact item SHIP_GATE.md:30 flags as PARTIAL and defers to Stage C.
findings.evidence:
SHIP_GATE.md:30 -- "`[cli]` No raw stack traces without `--debug` -- PARTIAL: ... there is no `--debug` flag yet and an *unexpected* exception still surfaces a Python traceback. Flagged as a Stage-C humanization item (top-level CLI handler + `--debug` opt-in)." Runtime probe `python -m ai_crucible characterize --items /nonexistent/path.json` produced:
  Traceback (most recent call last):
    File "...__main__.py", line 6, in <module>
      raise SystemExit(main())
    File "...cli.py", line 65, in main
      from ai_crucible.characterize.run import main as characterize_main
    ...
  ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'scipy'
cli.py:50-70 main() has no try/except around `return characterize_main(rest)`; cli.py:19-22 is the only try/except in the file.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Add a top-level wrapper in main() around the dispatch (and ideally the lazy import): catch Exception, and if the exception's str already carries the structured [CODE] message (hint: ...) shape (CalibrationLoadError, OllamaUnreachableError, OllamaBadResponseError, etc.), write just that one line to stderr and return a non-zero exit code; otherwise emit a generic structured wrapper ([CLI_UNEXPECTED] ... (hint: re-run with --debug for the full traceback)). Add a `--debug`/`-v` top-level flag (parsed in the dispatcher before forwarding) that re-raises / prints the full traceback for developers. SealedBoundaryViolation/ChromeAccessError/_GradingStageError already propagate unwrapped in the kernel for the same operator-vs-developer reason -- mirror that contrast here. Keep KeyboardInterrupt passing through to a clean 130.

findings.title: Exit-code contract (0 / 2 / characterize's 1) is not discoverable by the operator from --help or docs
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: An operator wiring `ai-crucible characterize` into a CI gate or the dogfood-swarm harness has to read the source to learn that 0=success, 2=unknown command, and 1=total-model-failure (CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS). Worst case they treat any non-zero as 'unknown command' or any non-2 as success and silently consume an empty/degraded report. The codes ARE stable and well-chosen (the comment at run.py:672 deliberately keeps 1 distinct from the dispatcher's 2) -- the gap is purely discoverability, so LOW.
findings.description: The CLI implements a clean, deliberate exit-code contract: cli.main returns 0 for success/help/version, 2 for unknown command (cli.py:70), and forwards characterize's code; characterize returns 1 on total model failure with a CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS structured stderr error (run.py:673-688). SHIP_GATE.md:29 records this as the [cli] Exit codes item. But none of this is surfaced to the operator: the --help options block (cli.py:44-47) lists only -V/--version and -h/--help; there is no `exit codes:` section in the banner and no mention in --help that characterize exits non-zero (and emits a coded JSON error) when every model fails.
findings.evidence:
cli.py:44-47 options block:
  options:
    -V, --version  print the installed version and exit
    -h, --help     show this message and exit
-- no exit-code documentation. cli.py:70 `return 2` (unknown command) and run.py:688 `return 1` (CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS) are undocumented to the operator. SHIP_GATE.md:29 notes the contract exists but the only place it is written down is the gate checklist, not operator-facing --help.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Add a short `exit codes:` section to the _usage() banner: 0 success; 2 unknown command; (characterize) 1 = no judgments collected (every model failed -- see the [CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS] stderr error and its hint). Optionally cross-reference from the README/handbook automation section so CI authors can gate on it.

domains.coverage_notes:
Scope covered in full: src/ai_crucible/cli.py, src/ai_crucible/__main__.py, src/ai_crucible/__init__.py, tests/test_cli.py. Traced the exception-propagation chain end-to-end into the dispatch target (characterize/run.py) and the downstream structured-error sources it can surface (calibration/loader.py CalibrationLoadError, models/ollama_adapter.py OllamaUnreachableError/OllamaBadResponseError) to determine exactly which faults reach the operator as a raw traceback vs. as a structured exit. Confirmed against SHIP_GATE.md (lines 26-34, the B. Error Handling block) and the Stage-A/Stage-B reports under swarm/. Ran a live CLI probe (python -m ai_crucible characterize --items /nonexistent/path.json) which reproduced a raw multi-frame traceback (deps not installed in this sandbox, so it surfaced ModuleNotFoundError: scipy via the cli.py:65 lazy import -- same escape path as a config error would take), directly substantiating cli-operator-001.

Deliberately NOT reported (either out of cli.py scope, or already handled, or correct as-is):
- Top finding (001) is bounded to MEDIUM not HIGH: the structured [CODE]+hint text IS present as the traceback's final line, and the most common operational failure -- Ollama down for the whole run -- is ALREADY handled cleanly via run_panel's per-model except -> CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS structured exit-1 (run.py:673-688). The operator is not left with no path forward; they are shown a noisy path.
- PROVISIONAL caveat surfacing: the load-bearing circular-omega/provisional-seat caveat IS surfaced to the human -- in the --help banner itself (cli.py:33-35) and in the emitted report (run.py:615 alt_test_caveat, 623-643 kappa_baseline.provisional=True). Not a CLI finding. (The CLI's own stdout run-summary -- 'seated:', 'composed panel (escalate/quorum)' at run.py:690-696 -- does not REPEAT the provisional caveat, but that summary lives in run.py, not my cli.py scope, and the caveat is not buried: it is in --help and in the report the operator reads. Flagging for the run.py/result-legibility domain owner rather than claiming it here.)
- Progress feedback during the ~2.6h run: run_panel prints per-model progress lines (run.py:355-358 'judged N records acc=... (Ns)', 411-414 seat decision per model, plus per-item drop lines 202). This is real progress feedback and it lives in run.py, outside cli.py scope -- defer to the run.py domain owner for any ETA/which-item granularity gap.
- Unknown-command UX (cli.py:69-70): writes 'ai-crucible: unknown command X' + the full usage to stderr and exits 2. This is helpful, not a defect -- intentionally chose not to report it.
- Consistency: the structured-error shape [CODE] message (hint: ...) is consistent across the modules the CLI can surface (loader.py _fail, ollama_adapter OllamaUnreachableError, puzzle.py PuzzleLoadError, attestation HashChainError, instrument SUTError/PreregError). No drift found at the CLI boundary; the only consistency gap is that cli.py's own top level does not yet PRESERVE that shape when an exception reaches it (covered by 001).

findings.title: The load-bearing PROVISIONAL caveat (circular-ω, all seats provisional, escalate-below-quorum) is written to the JSON report but NEVER printed to the human running the command
findings.line: 690-704 (stdout summary) vs 614-643 (caveat only in report dict); also 71-79 (_ALT_TEST_CAVEAT)
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: After a ~2.6h run the operator reads the terminal summary -- 'seated: [...]' and 'composed panel (quorum met): [...]' -- and walks away believing the panel is admitted/authoritative. The single most important honesty caveat of the whole instrument -- that on the default (no --human-labels) path the alt-test ω is a CIRCULAR model-jury bootstrap, the κ baseline is FABRICATED (0.80 hardcoded), and every seat is PROVISIONAL until human labels exist -- is surfaced only inside characterization-report.json (alt_test_caveat / kappa_baseline.provisional). A human who does not open and parse the JSON is silently misled about the validity of the measurement: a provisional, self-refereed result is presented on screen as a clean seated panel. This is the exact 'provisional result presented as authoritative' HIGH trigger.
findings.description: main() prints a terminal summary at the end of a run: the seated list, the composed-panel verdict (quorum met / escalate), and 'report -> <path>'. None of these lines mention that the run was on the model-jury-bootstrap reference, that ω is circular, that the κ baseline is fabricated, or that the seats are provisional. The caveat exists and is well-written -- _ALT_TEST_CAVEAT and the kappa_baseline.provisional=True block -- but it lives ONLY in the report dict that gets json.dumps'd to disk. The stdout path a human actually watches has zero reference to it. The 'quorum met' line in particular reads as an authoritative green result. (The CLI --help banner does carry a provisional note, but that is the pre-run banner, not the post-run result the operator reads to decide whether to trust the seats.)
findings.evidence: run.py:690-696 -- `seated = [...]; print(f"\nseated: {seated}"); comp = report["panel_composition"]; ... verdict = "escalate (sub-quorum)" if comp["escalate"] else "quorum met"; print(f"composed panel ({verdict}): {panel}")` -- no caveat printed. Contrast run.py:614-615 where the caveat is placed only into the report dict: `"alt_test_reference": "human" if human_labels else "model-jury-bootstrap", "alt_test_caveat": ... if human_labels else _ALT_TEST_CAVEAT,`.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: In main(), after the seated/composed-panel prints and on the default (human_labels is None) path, print a prominent multi-line PROVISIONAL banner to stdout/stderr: e.g. '*** PROVISIONAL RESULT: alt-test ω is a circular model-jury bootstrap and the κ baseline is hardcoded (0.80, not human-measured). All seat decisions are PROVISIONAL until a human-labeling round runs (--human-labels). Do not treat this seated panel as authoritative. See alt_test_caveat in <report>. ***'. Keep it the LAST thing printed so it is what the operator sees. On the human-grounded path, print the retired-caveat note instead.

findings.title: A DEGRADED run (failed models) is flagged in the report JSON but the operator's terminal summary never says 'this result is partial/degraded'
findings.line: 613 (degraded flag into report) vs 690-704 (summary prints, no degraded mention)
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: One of the six default-panel models OOMs or its tag is unservable mid-run. run_panel correctly captures it in `failed` and main() sets report['degraded']=True and lists failed_models -- but the terminal summary the operator reads prints only 'seated: [...]' and 'composed panel (quorum met): [...]'. The per-model ERROR line scrolled past hours earlier in a multi-hour log. The operator concludes a full 6-model panel was characterized when in fact it was a thinned 5-model run; a partial panel that happened to still meet quorum is consumed as a full-strength result. The exit code stays 0 (correctly, since it is not a total failure), so nothing else flags it. Capped at MEDIUM because the data IS in the report and the run did produce a usable (if thinned) result -- the operator is under-informed, not left stuck with no path forward, and a careful operator who reads the report or scrollback can recover the truth.
findings.description: Stage B added the honesty machinery: failed models are surfaced in `failed`, report['degraded'] is set, attempted_panel is recorded, and a per-model 'ERROR' line prints when the failure happens. But the END-of-run summary -- the lines an operator reliably reads after walking away for 2.6h -- never restates 'DEGRADED: N of M models failed (...)'. The degraded state is only legible to a JSON consumer or someone who scrolls back through hours of log to find the ERROR line. The human-facing summary presents a thinned panel identically to a clean one.
findings.evidence: run.py:613 `"degraded": bool(failed),` -- only in the report dict. run.py:690-704 the entire stdout summary (`seated`, `composed panel (verdict)`, `report -> ...`) makes no reference to `failed` or `degraded`. The earlier per-model failure print at run.py:316 `print(f"[{model_id}] ERROR: {exc!r}")` fires at failure time, potentially hours before the summary, and is easily lost in scrollback.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: In main(), before/with the seated summary, if `failed` (or report['degraded']) is truthy, print a clear line: 'DEGRADED RUN: {len(failed)} of {len(attempted_panel)} models failed and were dropped: {[f["model_id"] for f in failed]} -- this panel is PARTIAL. See failed_models in <report>.' so the degraded state is the last thing the operator sees, not buried in the report and old scrollback.

findings.title: No progress feedback during the multi-hour run: the operator gets silence per model until each ~25-minute pass finishes (and no per-item progress within a pass)
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: The operator launches `ai-crucible characterize` (default 6 models x ~120 items x k=3, a ~2.6h job). run_panel sets t0 then immediately blocks in collect_records for one model's entire pass (~25 min) before the FIRST print fires (the post-pass 'judged N records' line). Within a healthy pass, collect_records prints ONLY on a dropped item -- a clean item produces nothing. So for ~25 minutes at a stretch the terminal shows no output at all. With no per-item counter, no current-model 'now loading X' line, and no ETA, the operator cannot distinguish 'making progress' from 'hung on a stuck Ollama load'. The realistic consequence is a wrongly-killed run (operator assumes it crashed) or hours of anxious uncertainty -- wasted compute and time. Capped at MEDIUM: it is a missing-progress-output problem, not a correctness or stuck-with-no-path problem (the run does eventually print and finish; per-item TIMEOUTS from Stage B do bound true hangs).
findings.description: The only stdout during a run is: a per-item line ONLY when an item is dropped/times out (collect_records:202), and a single post-pass line per model AFTER its whole ~25-min pass completes (run_panel:355). There is no 'starting model i/N: <id>' announcement before a pass begins, no 'item j/total' counter within a pass, and no elapsed/ETA. For a healthy model the operator sees a ~25-minute black hole, then one summary line, repeated 6 times. A long-running diagnostic instrument that the docstrings themselves call a 'multi-hour run' (ollama_adapter.py:54) gives the operator no way to confirm liveness or estimate completion.
findings.evidence: run.py:307-327 -- the loop body is `model = OllamaModel(...); t0 = time.monotonic(); try: recs, stats = await collect_records(model, items, k=k)` with NO print before collect_records; the first feedback is run.py:355 `print(f"[{model_id}] judged {len(recs)} records  acc=... ({elapsed:.0f}s)")` which only runs AFTER the full pass. Inside collect_records (run.py:183-228) the success path appends a record with no print; the only print (run.py:202) is on the failure/drop branch.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Add a pre-pass announcement in run_panel before collect_records: `print(f"[{i+1}/{len(panel)}] loading + judging {model_id} ({len(items)} items x k={k}) ...", flush=True)`. Optionally thread a lightweight progress callback into collect_records to print 'item j/{n}' every N items (flush=True). Cheap, no new deps, and converts the ~25-min silence into visible liveness.

findings.title: The run never announces up front what it will do or roughly how long it will take
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: A first-time operator types `ai-crucible characterize` and the process appears to hang -- main() goes straight from argparse into asyncio.run(run_panel(...)) with zero output. There is no 'about to characterize 6 models over 120 items (k=3); expect ~2-3 hours; report -> characterization-report.json' line. The operator cannot tell whether the command even started, what panel/item-set it picked up (DEFAULT_PANEL vs --models, admission_pairs.json vs --items), or that they just committed to a multi-hour job. Realistic consequence: the operator Ctrl-C's a job they think is broken, or unknowingly kicks off a 2.6h run they did not intend (e.g. wanted a quick smoke test). MEDIUM: a missing-up-front-summary nicety; combined with finding 003 it is the difference between a respectful long job and a black box, but no data is lost and the run is recoverable.
findings.description: main() resolves items/panel/human_labels then immediately enters the blocking multi-hour run with no echo of the plan. The operator is never told, before the wait begins, the panel size, the item count, k, the item-set name, the reference mode (model-jury vs human), or the rough expected duration -- all of which are known at that point and all of which are exactly the information that lets a human decide 'yes, I meant to start a 2.6h job' vs 'wait, that's the wrong set, kill it now' BEFORE sinking the time.
findings.evidence: run.py:594-600 -- `items = load_items(...) if args.items else _load_admission_items(); panel = _parse_models(...) if args.models else DEFAULT_PANEL; human_labels = ...; profiles, records, failed = asyncio.run(run_panel(panel, items, k=args.k, human_labels=human_labels))` -- no print between resolving the plan and launching the multi-hour run. The 'n_items'/'k'/'item_set'/'attempted_panel' values are only assembled into the report dict AFTER the run (run.py:602-611).
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Right before the asyncio.run call, print a plan banner: `print(f"characterizing {len(panel)} model(s) over {len(items)} item(s), k={args.k}, item_set={...}, reference={'human' if human_labels else 'model-jury-bootstrap (PROVISIONAL)'}; this is a long run (minutes per model). report -> {args.out}", flush=True)`. Surfaces the plan + the provisional reference + the destination before the operator commits the time.

findings.title: The on-screen result is a one-line model summary; the WHY behind each seat/screen/reject (the contrastive notes) is legible only inside the JSON, not the terminal
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: build_profile invests heavily in human-legible, contrastive `notes` explaining exactly which gate moved each decision ('DECISION: REJECT -- κ<floor one-sided floor', 'DECISION: SEAT -- ... (review_flag set: super-consistent, Tier-1B)', the [degraded]/[warning] partial-set notes). On screen the operator gets only a terse `[model] reject  acc=...  q=...  ece=...  omega=...` line per model, and a final `seated: [...]`. The WHY -- the single most useful thing for an operator deciding whether a reject is a real signal or a format break -- is emitted only into report['profiles'][m]['notes']. The operator must open and navigate a large JSON to learn why a model was rejected. LOW: the information fully exists and is reachable; this is a 'could surface the reasons more directly' polish item, not a case of being stuck or misled.
findings.description: The decision rationale is excellent but lives in the report. The terminal shows numbers (acc/q/ece/omega) and a bare decision word per model, plus the seated list. Notably the per-model one-liner does NOT echo the most operator-relevant flags it already knows about -- e.g. the [warning] high-unparsed-rate note (run.py:403-407) and the [degraded] partial-item-set note (run.py:398-402) are appended to profile.notes but the stdout line only shows the raw `unparsed=`/`dropped_items=` counts at collect time, not the 'check the prompt format before trusting this decision' interpretation. So a REJECT driven by a format break looks identical on screen to a REJECT driven by a genuinely weak judge unless the operator opens the JSON.
findings.evidence: run.py:409-414 -- `flag = " [review]" if profile.metadata.get("review_flag") else ""; q = profile.metadata.get("quality_score"); print(f"[{model_id}] {profile.seat_decision.value}{flag}  acc={profile.objective_accuracy}  q={q}  ece={profile.ece}  omega={profile.alt_test_omega}")` -- numbers + decision word only; the contrastive `profile.notes` (built in profile.py:430-577, e.g. 'DECISION: REJECT -- {reason}') are never printed.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: In the per-model print (or the final summary), for any non-SEAT decision append the one-line reason from notes (the 'DECISION: ...' note) and surface the [warning]/[degraded] notes inline, e.g. `print(f"[{model_id}] {decision} -- {decision_reason}")`. Cheap, reuses existing notes, and makes the WHY legible without opening the report.

domains.coverage_notes: SCOPE COVERED (read in full): src/ai_crucible/characterize/run.py, profile.py, aggregate.py, panel_store.py + tests/test_characterize_run.py, test_characterize.py. Also read for the operator-facing path: src/ai_crucible/cli.py (the dispatcher + --help banner), src/ai_crucible/models/ollama_adapter.py (confirms the 'multi-hour run' framing + per-item timeout from Stage B). LENS APPLIED: result-legibility, progress-feedback, first-run/empty-state, consistency, on the operator-facing RESULT + PROGRESS of a characterization run. Did NOT re-report Stage A/B items (the structured errors, retry/backoff, per-item salvage/timeout, degraded-capture machinery, the CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS structured error + exit codes) -- those are present and well-built; my findings are strictly about the bridge from 'the report has the data' to 'the HUMAN watching the terminal sees it'. KEY PATTERN across findings 001/002/005: the honesty work Stage B did is real but lives in the JSON report dict; the stdout summary the operator actually reads after a 2.6h walk-away does not restate the provisional caveat (001, HIGH -- silently-misled trigger), the degraded flag (002), or the per-decision WHY (005). Findings 003/004 are the progress/announce gap on the multi-hour run. SEVERITY HONESTY: only 001 is HIGH (provisional/degraded-aware: a self-refereed provisional result is presented on screen as 'quorum met' with no caveat -> operator misled about measurement validity). 002/003/004 are MEDIUM (under-informed / black-box, but data exists and run is recoverable). 005 is LOW (polish; reasons exist in JSON). NOT A CLEAN DOMAIN, but the underlying instrument is sound -- every gap is a 'surface the existing truth to the human' fix, not a missing capability. Could not execute a live run (no Ollama panel in this read-only audit) to observe real stdout timing, but the print placement in run_panel/collect_records makes the silence behavior unambiguous from the source.

findings.title: Operator-input errors (--items / --human-labels) leak as a raw Python traceback — the structured [CODE]+hint is buried in a stack dump, no clean exit code
findings.line: run.py:594-600 (load_items / load_human_labels called outside any try); cli.py:62-67 (dispatch with no guard); __main__.py:6
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: A first-run operator who points the 2.6h job at a wrong path, a typo'd directory, or a malformed human_labels.json (e.g. `ai-crucible characterize --items ./my-items` where the dir is empty, or `--human-labels labels.json` with a bad annotator) gets a multi-frame Python traceback (`Traceback (most recent call last): ... raise SystemExit(main()) ... CalibrationLoadError: [INPUT_DIR_EMPTY] ...`). The carefully-authored actionable message exists, but it is the LAST line of a stack dump that reads as a crash/bug, not an actionable config error. There is no --debug flag to opt INTO the traceback, so every operator gets the raw form. The process also exits with Python's default unhandled-exception code, not the deliberate exit-1 the no-judgments path uses, so a CI/automation gate cannot distinguish a config error from an internal crash. This is the exact gap Ship-Gate B closed everywhere ELSE: the loader modules raise a perfect `[CODE] message (hint: ...)`, but nothing on the CLI path catches it and prints `stderr: <message>` + `return 1`.
findings.description: The structured-error surface is excellent module-by-module (loader.py, human_labels.py, puzzle.py, etc. all raise `[CODE] message (hint: ...)`), but the CLI entry path never CATCHES those exceptions and converts them into the clean operator-facing form. `cli.main` -> `characterize.run.main` -> `load_items(args.items)` / `load_human_labels(...)` / `_parse_models(...)` all execute as bare top-level statements (run.py lines 594-596) before the `run_panel` per-model try/except. A `CalibrationLoadError` / `HumanLabelError` (both of which carry the structured hint) therefore propagates unhandled through `cli.main` (no try) and `__main__.py:6` (`raise SystemExit(main())`), surfacing as a raw traceback. Contrast: the same module's `CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS` path (run.py:673-688) does exactly the right thing — writes a `{code,message,hint}` JSON to stderr and `return 1`. The pre-run loader errors deserve identical treatment. The CLI is the ONLY place the operator meets these errors, so this is where the polish has to land. (test_cli.py covers --version/--help/unknown-command but has no test for a bad --items/--human-labels path, which is why the gap survived Stage B.)
findings.evidence:
run.py:594  `items = load_items(args.items) if args.items else _load_admission_items()`
run.py:596  `human_labels = load_human_labels(args.human_labels, items) if args.human_labels else None`
— neither wrapped in try/except; cli.py:62-67 dispatches with no guard; __main__.py:6 `raise SystemExit(main())`. The structured messages that get buried, e.g. loader.py:107-111 `raise _fail("INPUT_PATH_MISSING", f"calibration path does not exist: {root}", "pass a path to a .json file or a directory of .json files")` and human_labels.py:152-156 `raise _fail("INPUT_PATH_MISSING", f"human-labels path does not exist: {root}", "pass a path to a human_labels.json file")`.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Wrap the operator path in one top-level handler that mirrors the existing CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS shape. Simplest: in run.py main(), wrap the `load_items` / `load_human_labels` / `_parse_models` block (or the whole body) in `try: ... except (CalibrationLoadError, HumanLabelError, SUTError, PuzzleLoadError) as exc: sys.stderr.write(str(exc) + "\n"); return 1`. Better: a thin handler in cli.main() that catches the repo's structured-error base types, writes the already-formatted `[CODE] message (hint: ...)` string to stderr (no stack), and returns 1; gate the raw traceback behind a `--debug` / `AI_CRUCIBLE_DEBUG=1` opt-in. Add a test_cli/test_characterize_run case asserting a bad `--items` path returns 1 with the `[INPUT_...]` message on stderr and NO `Traceback` text.

findings.title: End-of-run stdout omits the PROVISIONAL caveat and the DEGRADED flag — the operator reads `seated: [...]` as an authoritative result
findings.line: run.py:690-703 (final stdout block); caveat/degraded only in the JSON report at 613-615
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: After a ~2.6h local run the operator watches the terminal, which prints `seated: ['qwen3.6:27b', 'mistral-small:24b', ...]` and `composed panel (quorum met): [...]`. The two load-bearing honesty signals — that on the default (no --human-labels) path the seats are PROVISIONAL because alt-test omega is a CIRCULAR model-jury bootstrap, and that the run was DEGRADED if any model failed mid-run — are written ONLY into the JSON report file, never echoed to stdout. An operator who acts on the terminal output (commits the panel, reports 'judges seated') without opening and grepping the JSON is silently misled into treating a provisional/circular measurement as a human-grounded, authoritative one. This is the precise 'provisional result presented as authoritative' risk the lens flags — moderated to MEDIUM (not HIGH) because the caveat IS faithfully recorded in the persisted report, the `escalate (sub-quorum)` verdict IS printed, and per-model `[model] ERROR:` lines do scroll past during the run; the operator is not left with NO path to the truth, just a too-easy path to missing it.
findings.description: `run.py` does meticulous honesty work in the JSON: `degraded: bool(failed)` (line 613), `alt_test_caveat: _ALT_TEST_CAVEAT` (line 615, the loud 'model-jury bootstrap / seats PROVISIONAL' string), and `kappa_baseline.provisional: True`. But the human-facing stdout tail (lines 690-703) prints only `seated: [...]`, the composed-panel line (which correctly surfaces `escalate (sub-quorum)`), and `report -> path`. There is no stdout line that says 'SEATS PROVISIONAL — model-jury bootstrap, not human-grounded (pass --human-labels for a real alt-test)' and no 'RUN DEGRADED — N model(s) failed: [...]' summary. The degraded signal in particular is only discoverable by scrolling back to the mid-run `[model] ERROR:` prints or opening the JSON. For a multi-hour headless job, the terminal tail IS the result for most operators.
findings.evidence: run.py:691 `print(f"\nseated: {seated}")`  run.py:696 `print(f"composed panel ({verdict}): {panel}")`  run.py:703 `print(f"report -> {args.out}")` — no print of `_ALT_TEST_CAVEAT` or `report['degraded']`. Compare the JSON-only `_ALT_TEST_CAVEAT` (run.py:71-79): 'Seat decisions are PROVISIONAL until human labels exist (§12 Q3).'
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: After the `seated:` print, when `human_labels is None` echo a one-line provisional banner to stdout/stderr, e.g. `print('NOTE: seats are PROVISIONAL — alt-test omega is a circular model-jury bootstrap, not human-grounded; pass --human-labels for a non-circular alt-test (see alt_test_caveat in the report).')`. And when `report['degraded']` is true, print a summary line `print(f'RUN DEGRADED: {len(failed)} model(s) failed: {[f["model_id"] for f in failed]} — the seated panel is thinner than requested')`. This surfaces the two caveats the JSON already carries to the human watching the terminal, with zero change to the (already-correct) report contents.

findings.title: Sealed-boundary / chrome-leak HALT errors drift from the [CODE]+hint shape — they name the leak precisely but the remediation is implicit and they carry no stable code
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: When the sealed boundary fires — the single most important eval-integrity HALT in the system, the one that says 'this attempt's measurement is contaminated' — the developer embedding ai_crucible gets a message that names WHAT leaked (token, message index, role) and WHY it matters, but with no stable `[CODE]` to grep/branch on and no explicit `(hint: ...)` naming the next action (keep rank/leaderboard/standings in AttemptState.chrome; never interpolate a chrome value into a scored message). Every other structured error in the codebase carries a code + hint, so a developer who has tooling keyed on the `[CODE]` prefix (or who expects the hint) finds the most critical error is the odd one out. Worst realistic outcome is slower remediation of a contamination bug, not a stuck/misled operator — and these fire on the LIBRARY-embedding path (the headless CLI characterize flow does not build chrome), so the blast radius is developers, not the characterization operator. Hence LOW.
findings.description: `SealedBoundaryViolation` (engagement.py) and `ChromeAccessError` (roles.py) are the chrome-leak andon halts. Both raise a clear, accurate prose message that names the offending token/role and gestures at the principle ('Motivation must stay in chrome; the scored context must remain framing-neutral' / 'rank/leaderboard/standings must never enter messages'). But unlike the rest of the repo's structured errors, they do NOT use a `_fail(code, message, hint)` helper: there is no `[CODE]` prefix and no explicit `(hint: ...)` clause telling the developer the concrete fix. This is the one place the otherwise-uniform error shape drifts. The kernel's `_degrade_into_error` wraps OTHER stage failures into `[GRADING_FAILED]...(hint:...)`, but sealed-boundary violations are intentionally re-raised UNwrapped (kernel.py:652) so they reach the caller in their raw form — which is exactly the form that lacks code+hint.
findings.evidence: engagement.py:209-214 `raise SealedBoundaryViolation(f"Tier-3 chrome value {token!r} leaked into scored context (message[{index}], role={role!r}). Motivation must stay in chrome; the scored context must remain deployment-shaped and framing-neutral (research-grounding §10.1(d,e)).")` — no `[CODE]`, no `(hint:`. roles.py:110-113 `raise ChromeAccessError("Tier-3 chrome leaked into the scored context (§10.1(e)): rank/leaderboard/standings must never enter messages")`.
findings.proposed_fix_sketch: Bring these two into the house shape without changing their HALT semantics: prefix a stable code and append a hint, e.g. `[SEALED_BOUNDARY_LEAK] Tier-3 chrome value {token!r} leaked into scored context (message[{index}], role={role!r}) (hint: keep rank/leaderboard/standings/prizes in AttemptState.chrome only; never interpolate a chrome value into a scored message — see build_scored_context / assert_no_chrome_leak)`. Same for ChromeAccessError with `[CHROME_ACCESS]`. Keep them as their existing exception subclasses (so the kernel's unwrapped re-raise still works); only the message string changes. This is the smallest possible nudge to consistency for the most-load-bearing error.

findings.title: Exit codes (0 / 1 / 2) are not documented to the operator anywhere reachable from --help
findings.worst_realistic_consequence: An operator wiring `ai-crucible characterize` into a script or CI gate has to read the source to learn that 0 = success, 1 = ran-but-collected-zero-judgments (CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS / invalid report), and 2 = unknown command. The exit-code contract IS deliberate and well-chosen (the run.py comment at 672 explicitly notes '1 is distinct from the dispatcher's 2'), but it is invisible to the operator: the `--help` usage banner lists options but not exit codes, and there is no man-page/README line the CLI points to. Worst case is a CI gate that treats any non-zero as a generic failure and cannot act differently on 'config error' vs 'every model down' vs 'unknown command' — a missed-opportunity, not a stuck state. LOW.
findings.description: The usage banner (`_usage`, cli.py:25-47) documents commands and `-V`/`-h` but says nothing about what the process returns. The codebase has a clear, intentional exit-code scheme (0 success, 1 ran-but-empty/invalid, 2 unknown-command) but it lives only in code + comments. For a headless instrument explicitly designed to be gated by automation/the dogfood-swarm harness (per run.py:672 comment), the exit-code contract is part of the operator-facing API and should be discoverable without reading source.
findings.evidence: cli.py:_usage() (lines 30-47) ends at `-h, --help  show this message and exit` with no 'exit codes:' section. The scheme: cli.py:56 `return 0`, cli.py:70 `return 2` (unknown command), run.py:688 `return 1` (CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS), run.py:704 `return 0`. run.py:672 comment: 'Exit 1 is distinct from the dispatcher's 2 (unknown command).'
findings.proposed_fix_sketch:
Add a short `exit codes:` block to the `_usage` banner (or to the `ai-crucible characterize --help` argparse epilog), e.g. `exit codes:
  0  success
  1  ran but collected zero judgments / invalid report (e.g. every model unreachable)
  2  usage error (unknown command)`. Optionally mirror it in the README so it is reachable both ways. Pure documentation — no behavior change.

domains.coverage_notes:
Read-only sweep of the structured-error + hint surface across all of src/ai_crucible/**.py. Catalogued every exception class (20) and every `raise` site in src/ (~150). VERDICT: the structured-error surface is genuinely strong and largely CLEAN — Stage B did real, uniform work. Eleven modules (calibration/loader.py, calibration/irt.py, characterize/human_labels.py, characterize/panel_store.py, puzzle.py, attestation.py, instrument/prereg.py, instrument/sut.py, instrument/tuning.py, instrument/inspect_task.py, instrument/rubric_bundle.py) all use an IDENTICAL `_fail(code, message, hint)` helper emitting `[CODE] message (hint: ...)` — exemplary consistency. The Ollama adapter errors (OllamaUnreachableError/ModelMismatchError/OllamaBadResponseError) carry code+host+model+hint. The kernel's `_degrade_into_error` produces `[GRADING_FAILED]...(hint:...)` and preserves the transcript. The CLI's CHARACTERIZE_NO_JUDGMENTS path (run.py) writes a clean {code,message,hint} JSON + exit 1.

Deliberately NOT flagged as defects: (a) the many bare `raise ValueError(...)` in scoring/stats.py, characterize/metrics.py, characterize/aggregate.py, observability.py, types.py, scoring/judge_panel.py — these are programming-contract guards on internal/library functions (n<1, empty list, threshold out of range) whose operator-supplied inputs (--k, --epsilon) are either caught by argparse `type=int` or already validated upstream in human_labels.py before reaching them; they are appropriately developer-facing and name the bad value. (b) budget.py BudgetExceeded — these are NOT operator errors to 'fix' but the EXPECTED terminal state of an attempt (budget/time/hard-kill); the kernel stamps terminated_by and it becomes a legitimate measurement outcome, so a 'raise the budget' hint would be wrong. (c) sandbox.py path-escape PermissionError and exec ValueError — internal security/contract guards, accurate and named.

The EXTERNAL_VERIFIER empty-panel ValueErrors in judge_panel.py (lines 439, 474) name the offending generator family and the cross-family requirement but lack a [CODE]/explicit hint; I judged these BELOW the reporting bar because (1) they are caught by the kernel grading seam and re-surfaced through the well-built `_degrade_into_error` `[GRADING_FAILED]...(hint:...)` path, so the operator never sees the bare form, and (2) they are developer-facing library-internal contracts. The sealed-boundary equivalents (SealedBoundaryViolation, ChromeAccessError) ARE re-raised unwrapped, so I flagged those as 003.

Environment note: could not run the CLI end-to-end here (scipy not installed in the probe interpreter), but the absence of any top-level try/except on the cli.main -> run.main path is unambiguous by static reading (finding 001). Findings 002 (caveat/degraded not on stdout) and 003 (sealed-boundary shape drift) were cross-checked against the prompt's RESULT-LEGIBILITY and CONSISTENCY lenses respectively. Four findings total: 1 HIGH, 1 MEDIUM, 2 LOW.
